---
name: review-phase
description: Review code changes from a completed phase against the contract (reviewer role)
context: fork
agent: reviewer
---

<role>Du bist der REVIEWER. Du pruefst Aenderungen gegen den Contract und gibst ein PASS/FAIL Verdict. Du aenderst KEINEN Code.</role>

<instructions>
<step id="1">Read the contract from `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` to understand the acceptance criteria.</step>
<step id="1b">If `.brv/context-tree/` exists and `brv` CLI is available, run `brv query "What domain rules and patterns apply to phase {PHASE_ID}?"` to understand domain expectations the code should meet.</step>
<step id="2">Run `git diff HEAD~{COMMITS}` to see all changes made during this phase. Examine each changed file.</step>
<step id="3">For each acceptance criterion, EXECUTE the verification method described in the criterion. Do NOT judge pass/fail by reading code alone — you MUST run the actual command and record the output as evidence.</step>
<step id="4">Check for code quality issues: bugs, broken API compatibility, incomplete work, logic errors.</step>
<step id="5">If `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` exists, run the MANDATORY e2e verification steps described below. This is NOT optional.</step>
<step id="6">Determine the overall verdict using the strict rules below.</step>
<step id="7">Write the review JSON to `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` using the exact schema below.</step>
</instructions>

<e2e-verification hint="MANDATORY when ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json exists">
<step id="5a">Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` to get the test harness.</step>
<step id="5b">Run each health_check command to confirm the environment is alive. If a health check fails, note it as a finding but do not FAIL the review for infrastructure issues.</step>
<step id="5c">If auth.login_command is set, run it to obtain a token/session. Use it for subsequent requests.</step>
<step id="5d">Run each verification_command. Check if the result matches the "expected" field. If a verification command fails and it relates to an acceptance criterion, mark that criterion as "fail" and include the command output in evidence.</step>
<step id="5e">If e2e_framework.run_command is set and the framework is configured, run the e2e test suite. Report failures as findings with severity "critical".</step>
</e2e-verification>

<output file="${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json">
{
  "phase_id": "01",
  "iteration": 1,
  "verdict": "pass or fail",
  "criteria_results": [
    {
      "ac_id": "AC-01-01",
      "verdict": "pass",
      "notes": "Criterion met because X",
      "evidence": [
        {
          "command": "curl -sf http://localhost:3000/api/health",
          "exit_code": 0,
          "stdout_snippet": "{\"status\":\"ok\"}",
          "stderr_snippet": ""
        }
      ]
    },
    {
      "ac_id": "AC-01-02",
      "verdict": "fail",
      "notes": "Not met: expected Y but found Z",
      "evidence": [
        {
          "command": "npx playwright test --grep 'feature'",
          "exit_code": 1,
          "stdout_snippet": "1 failed\n  Error: expected 'Dashboard' but got 'Login'",
          "stderr_snippet": ""
        }
      ]
    }
  ],
  "findings": [
    {
      "severity": "critical",
      "file": "src/foo.py",
      "line": 42,
      "description": "Null check missing — will crash when input is None",
      "suggestion": "Add 'if x is None: return' guard"
    }
  ],
  "summary": "Overall assessment of the phase"
}
</output>

<rules>
<rule>The "verdict" field MUST be exactly "pass" or "fail" (lowercase)</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY criteria_results entry has verdict "fail"</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY finding has severity "critical"</rule>
<rule>Verdict is "fail" if ANY behavioral or boundary criterion has verdict "pass" but EMPTY evidence — you cannot declare pass without proof</rule>
<rule>Every criteria_results entry MUST reference an ac_id from the contract</rule>
<rule>Every behavioral/boundary criteria_results entry MUST include at least one evidence entry with the actual command run and its output — structural criteria may pass without evidence</rule>
<rule>Every finding MUST reference a specific file and line number</rule>
<rule>The "iteration" field must match the --iteration argument</rule>
<rule>The output MUST be valid JSON matching the schema exactly</rule>
<rule>If e2e verification commands fail, include the command output in the finding notes AND in the criterion evidence</rule>
<rule>Unit tests passing alone is NOT sufficient to mark a criterion as "pass" — the verification method in the contract must be executed</rule>
<rule>If the contract criterion says "Run: <command>" you MUST run that exact command and record the result</rule>
</rules>

<quality-checklist>
<check category="Bugs">None-checks, fehlende Parameter-Validierung, falsche Typen</check>
<check category="API-Kompatibilitaet">Werden bestehende Funktionssignaturen gebrochen?</check>
<check category="Unvollstaendige-Arbeit">TODOs, auskommentierter Code, fehlende Imports</check>
<check category="Logik-Fehler">Off-by-one, falsche Vergleiche, fehlende Edge Cases</check>
<check category="E2e-Verifikation">ALLE verification_commands aus test-harness.json ausfuehren — Ergebnisse als evidence aufnehmen</check>
</quality-checklist>

<constraints>
<constraint>Du bist NUR Reviewer. Aendere KEINEN Code.</constraint>
<constraint>Output ONLY the JSON file — no markdown, no commentary</constraint>
<constraint>The file path MUST be ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json</constraint>
<constraint>Do NOT spawn sub-agents or call the Agent tool. You are a single-pass reviewer — write the review file, validate it, and stop. Do NOT launch another reviewer for "iteration 2" or any other reason.</constraint>
<constraint>Infrastructure failures (health check down) are INFO findings, not CRITICAL — don't fail a review because a service is temporarily unavailable</constraint>
<constraint>A "pass" verdict without evidence for behavioral/boundary criteria is INVALID — the orchestrator will reject it. Structural criteria may pass based on build/lint results alone.</constraint>
<constraint>Verify, don't vibe — you MUST execute verification commands literally and record actual output as evidence. No pattern-matching, no "looks right to me." See `hi/playbook/canon/verify-dont-vibe.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>No sycophancy — if the executor's work is incorrect, say so with specific findings. Do not mark criteria pass to avoid conflict. See `hi/playbook/canon/no-sycophancy-rule.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>Scope discipline — flag unexpected scope expansion in the executor's diff as a finding. Changes outside the task's declared files_to_modify are suspect. See `hi/playbook/canon/scope-is-sacred.md`.</constraint>
<constraint>Scratch state hygiene — flag any committed files under `.dirigent/`, `.dirigent-onboarding/`, `.planning/`, or other scratch dirs as a critical finding. See `hi/playbook/canon/scratch-state-hygiene.md`.</constraint>
</constraints>

## Validation (MANDATORY)

After writing the review JSON, validate it:

```bash
python ${CLAUDE_SKILL_DIR}/scripts/validate_schema.py ${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json
```

If validation fails, fix the errors and re-run until it passes.

-<constraint>Infrastructure failures (health check down) are INFO findings, not CRITICAL — don't fail a review because a service is temporarily unavailable</constraint>

+<constraint>Infrastructure failures (service down, missing env vars, connection refused) → criterion verdict "warn" (not "fail") and INFO-level findings (not CRITICAL). Use "fail" only for genuine code defects where the code was executed and produced wrong results.</constraint>

<constraint>A "pass" verdict without evidence for behavioral/boundary criteria is INVALID — the orchestrator will reject it. Structural criteria may pass based on build/lint results alone.</constraint>

<constraint>Verify, don't vibe — you MUST execute verification commands literally and record actual output as evidence. No pattern-matching, no "looks right to me." See `hi/playbook/canon/verify-dont-vibe.md`.</constraint>

<constraint>No sycophancy — if the executor's work is incorrect, say so with specific findings. Do not mark criteria pass to avoid conflict. See `hi/playbook/canon/no-sycophancy-rule.md`.</constraint>

---
name: fix-review
description: Fix issues found during phase review (executor role)
context: fork
agent: implementer
---

<role>Du bist der EXECUTOR. Der Reviewer hat Findings in `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` dokumentiert. Deine Aufgabe: alle critical und warn Findings fixen.</role>

<instructions>
<step id="1">Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/reviews/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` to get the structured review with findings.</step>
<step id="2">Read `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/contracts/phase-{PHASE_ID}.json` to understand which acceptance criteria failed.</step>
<step id="2b">If `.brv/context-tree/` exists, run `brv query` with the finding descriptions to check for domain context that helps inform the fix approach.</step>
<step id="3">Fix all findings with severity "critical" first, then "warn". Skip "info" unless trivial.</step>
<step id="4">For each failed acceptance criterion (verdict "fail" in criteria_results), address the root cause noted in the "notes" field.</step>
<step id="5">Commit: `git add -A && git commit -m "fix(phase-{PHASE_ID}): review fixes iteration {N}"`</step>
</instructions>

<rules>
<rule>Fix findings in priority order: critical first, then warn</rule>
<rule>Each fix must be minimal and focused — do not change more than needed</rule>
<rule>No new features — only fix what the reviewer found</rule>
<rule>If no critical or warn findings exist, do nothing</rule>
<rule>Reference the ac_id when fixing a criterion-related issue</rule>
</rules>

<constraints>
<constraint>Keine neuen Features einfuehren — nur Fixes</constraint>
<constraint>Jeder Fix muss direkt auf ein Finding oder failed Criterion zurueckfuehrbar sein</constraint>
<constraint>Read before you write — never edit a file without reading it first in this session (see `hi/playbook/canon/read-before-you-write.md`)</constraint>
<constraint>Verify, don't vibe — every fix must be structurally checked (syntax/typecheck/lint) before commit; behavioral check if the app can run (see `hi/playbook/canon/verify-dont-vibe.md`)</constraint>
<constraint>No sycophancy — if a finding is wrong or the reviewer is mistaken, push back with evidence; do not silently agree and fabricate a fix (see `hi/playbook/canon/no-sycophancy-rule.md`)</constraint>
<constraint>Scope is sacred — scope drift during a fix iteration is doubly expensive; fix only the finding, nothing adjacent (see `hi/playbook/canon/scope-is-sacred.md`)</constraint>
<constraint>Scratch state hygiene — do not commit files from `.dirigent/`, `.planning/`, or other scratch dirs (see `hi/playbook/canon/scratch-state-hygiene.md`)</constraint>
</constraints>

content.text:
210 passed, 0 failures. All changes are in place:

1. **`contract_schema.py`** — Added `warned_criteria` and `infra_constrained_only` computed properties to `Review`
2. **`contract.py`** — Orchestrator promotes FAIL→PASS when all failures are WARN (infra-only); fix agent prompt skips WARN criteria
3. **`reviewer.md`** — Defined WARN criterion semantics (infra constraint, not code defect)
4. **`review-phase/SKILL.md`** — Updated constraint: infra failures → `"warn"` verdict + INFO findings
5. **`fix-review/SKILL.md`** — Step 4 and new rule: skip `"warn"` criteria entirely

The flow now: if a reviewer encounters HTTP 500 from missing env vars, it marks those criteria `"warn"` instead of `"fail"`. The orchestrator sees all-WARN → promotes to PASS with caveat → loop exits on first iteration instead of burning 3 iterations and blocking.

fix(review): promote WARN-only reviews to PASS instead of blocking pipeline

all 3 review iterations trying to fix code that wasn't broken. Now the
reviewer marks these as WARN instead of FAIL, and the orchestrator promotes
to PASS with caveat when all failures are infra-only.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

message.content: in one legacy refactor, we observed: 1) the llm chose greenield as path

in one legacy refactor, we observed: 1) the llm chose greenield as path 2) die Arhcitecture.md und das test-harness enthielten falcshe Infos über den Login-Endpunkt, due dann downstream geuztzt wurden und zu einer komplett falsch refactorten app gefpühr haben. Wäre das in der legacy-route auch passiert? wird dort auch arhcitecture.md geschribene?

in one legacy refactor, we observed: 1) the llm chose greenield as path 2) die Arhcitecture.md und das test-harness enthielten falcshe Infos über den Login-Endpunkt, due dann downstream geuztzt wurden und zu einer komplett falsch refactorten app gefpühr haben. Wäre das in der legacy-route auch passiert? wird dort auch arhcitecture.md geschribene? und wenn ha wann

input.description: Explore route definitions and architecture.md generation
input.prompt:
I need to understand how different routes (greenfield vs legacy vs hybrid) are defined in outbid-dirigent, and specifically when/where ARCHITECTURE.md and test-harness are generated.

Find:
1. The route definitions — where are the step sequences for greenfield, legacy, hybrid defined? What steps does each route contain?
2. Where/when is ARCHITECTURE.md generated? Which routes include it? Is it in the greenfield route, legacy route, or both?
3. Where/when is test-harness generated? Which routes include it?
4. How does the router decide between greenfield vs legacy?

Look in src/outbid_dirigent/router.py or similar. Report step sequences for each route with file paths and line numbers.

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have a complete picture. Let me create a comprehensive summary document with all the information the user needs:

Based on my exploration of the outbid-dirigent codebase, here's a comprehensive breakdown of how routes are defined and when ARCHITECTURE.md and test-harness are generated:

## 1. ROUTE DEFINITIONS

All route step sequences are defined in `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/router.py` in the `Router` class:

### **GREENFIELD_STEPS** (Lines 102-142)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap dev environment
2. GREENFIELD_SCAFFOLD (optional) — Propose test setup & architecture
3. PLANNING — Analyze repo & create execution plan
4. EXECUTION — Execute tasks with fresh context per task
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional) — Align docs, remove dead code
6. TEST (optional) — Run full test suite
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **LEGACY_STEPS** (Lines 144-183)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap dev environment
2. BUSINESS_RULE_EXTRACTION — Extract all business rules from codebase
3. PLANNING — Create plan using BUSINESS_RULES.md as context
4. EXECUTION — Execute tasks with business rule verification after each
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional) — Align docs, remove dead code
6. TEST (optional) — Run full test suite
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **HYBRID_STEPS** (Lines 185-224)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap dev environment
2. QUICK_SCAN — Analyze only feature-relevant files
3. PLANNING — Create plan with repo context
4. EXECUTION — Execute tasks with repo context
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional) — Align docs, remove dead code
6. TEST (optional) — Run full test suite
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **TESTABILITY_STEPS** (Lines 226-265)
```
1. INIT (required) — Bootstrap, assess current testability
2. INCREASE_TESTABILITY — Analyze gaps and produce recommendations
3. PLANNING — Plan testability improvements
4. EXECUTION — Implement test infrastructure
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional)
6. TEST (optional) — Verify new test infrastructure
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **TRACKING_STEPS** (Lines 267-311)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap, detect existing analytics
2. QUICK_SCAN — Identify key user flows to track
3. ADD_TRACKING — Install PostHog SDK
4. PLANNING — Plan event instrumentation
5. EXECUTION — Add PostHog initialization & tracking
6. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional)
7. TEST (optional) — Verify events fire correctly
8. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

---

## 2. ARCHITECTURE.MD GENERATION

**Where it's generated:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py`, lines 581-587

**When it's generated:** During the INIT step (which is optional and present in ALL routes except QUICK)

**Key code:**
```python
# Step 3b: Generate ARCHITECTURE.md if it doesn't exist
arch_path = self.repo_path / "ARCHITECTURE.md"
if not arch_path.exists() and self.runner:
    logger.info("No ARCHITECTURE.md found — generating one")
    self.runner._run_claude("Run /dirigent:generate-architecture", timeout=600)
elif arch_path.exists():
    logger.info("ARCHITECTURE.md exists — skipping generation")
```

**Which routes include it:**
- **GREENFIELD** — via optional INIT step (line 104-107)
- **LEGACY** — via optional INIT step (line 146-149)
- **HYBRID** — via optional INIT step (line 187-190)
- **TESTABILITY** — via REQUIRED INIT step (line 229-231)
- **TRACKING** — via optional INIT step (line 269-272)

**Important:** ARCHITECTURE.md is generated automatically in the INIT phase (`init_phase.run()`) and is **NOT part of any specific route step sequence**. It's generated unconditionally if it doesn't exist, regardless of which route is chosen. The skill invoked is `/dirigent:generate-architecture`.

---

## 3. TEST-HARNESS GENERATION

**Where it's generated:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py`, lines 569-576

**When it's generated:** During the INIT step, after running the init script (if one exists)

**Key code:**
```python
# Step 3: If no harness yet, have the skill build one
if harness is None and self.runner:
    logger.info("No test-harness.json — invoking /dirigent:run-init to build one")
    success, _, stderr = self.runner._run_claude(
        "Run /dirigent:run-init", timeout=300,
    )
    if success:
        harness = TestHarness.load(harness_path)
```

**Which routes include it:**
- **GREENFIELD** — via optional INIT step
- **LEGACY** — via optional INIT step
- **HYBRID** — via optional INIT step
- **TESTABILITY** — via REQUIRED INIT step
- **TRACKING** — via optional INIT step

**Important:** test-harness.json is generated during the INIT phase by the `/dirigent:run-init` skill, which:
1. Tries to run `.outbid/init.sh` or `init.sh` if it exists
2. If the init script produces test-harness.json directly, uses that
3. Otherwise, invokes the `/dirigent:run-init` skill to inspect the repo and build a best-effort harness

---

## 4. ROUTE DECISION LOGIC

**Location:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/analyzer.py`, lines 850-964

### **Primary routing: LLM-based** (lines 850-874)
First tries to use Claude via `/outbid_dirigent/llm_router.py` to decide the route. The LLM gets:
- Full spec content
- Commit count
- Test harness summary (if available)
- System prompt with route descriptions

### **Fallback routing: Heuristic** (lines 876-964)
If LLM routing fails, uses heuristic signals:

**Legacy signals (require ≥3-4 signals):**
- Repo inactive for >1 year (+2 signals)
- Language migration (e.g., Java→Python) (+3 signals)
- Legacy keywords in spec ("migrate", "refactor", etc.) (+1-2 signals)
- Large project (>2000 commits) (+1 signal)

**Greenfield signals (require ≥2-3 signals):**
- Greenfield keywords ("build", "create", "new", etc.) (+2 signals)
- Modern language (TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust) (+1 signal)
- Active project (<90 days since last commit) (+1 signal)
- Small project (<50 files) (+1 signal)

**Route selection:**
- `legacy` if legacy_signals ≥ 3-4
- `greenfield` if greenfield_signals ≥ 2-3
- `hybrid` as default

**File locations:**
- Route decision saved to `.dirigent/ROUTE.json` (line 380)
- LLM route decision saved to `.dirigent/LLM_ROUTE.json` (llm_router.py, line 152)
- Analysis result saved to `.dirigent/analysis.json` (analyzer.py, line 967+)

---

## 5. EXECUTION FLOW

The route is executed in `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/dirigent.py`, lines 345-492 in the `run_execution()` function:

For each step in `route.steps`:
1. Skip if already marked complete in STATE.json
2. Call the corresponding executor method:
   - `StepType.INIT` → `executor.run_init()` (line 367) ← **This generates ARCHITECTURE.md and test-harness.json**
   - `StepType.GREENFIELD_SCAFFOLD` → `executor.greenfield_scaffold()` (line 374)
   - `StepType.BUSINESS_RULE_EXTRACTION` → `executor.extract_business_rules()` (line 381)
   - `StepType.QUICK_SCAN` → `executor.quick_scan()` (line 388)
   - `StepType.PLANNING` → `executor.create_plan()` (line 415)
   - `StepType.EXECUTION` → `executor.execute_plan()` (line 456)
   - `StepType.ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION` → `executor.entropy_minimization()` (line 463)
   - `StepType.TEST` → `executor.run_tests()` (line 470)
   - `StepType.SHIP` → `executor.ship()` (line 477)
3. Mark step complete in STATE.json (line 484)
4. If step fails and is required, abort; if optional, continue

The actual execution methods are in `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/executor.py`.

### **HYBRID_STEPS** (Lines 185-224)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap dev environment
2. QUICK_SCAN — Analyze only feature-relevant files
3. PLANNING — Create plan with repo context
4. EXECUTION — Execute tasks with repo context
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional) — Align docs, remove dead code
6. TEST (optional) — Run full test suite
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **TESTABILITY_STEPS** (Lines 226-265)
```
1. INIT (required) — Bootstrap, assess current testability
2. INCREASE_TESTABILITY — Analyze gaps and produce recommendations
3. PLANNING — Plan testability improvements
4. EXECUTION — Implement test infrastructure
5. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional)
6. TEST (optional) — Verify new test infrastructure
7. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

### **TRACKING_STEPS** (Lines 267-311)
```
1. INIT (optional) — Bootstrap, detect existing analytics
2. QUICK_SCAN — Identify key user flows to track
3. ADD_TRACKING — Install PostHog SDK
4. PLANNING — Plan event instrumentation
5. EXECUTION — Add PostHog initialization & tracking
6. ENTROPY_MINIMIZATION (optional)
7. TEST (optional) — Verify events fire correctly
8. SHIP — Create branch, push, PR
```

---

## 2. ARCHITECTURE.MD GENERATION

**Where it's generated:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py`, lines 581-587

**When it's generated:** During the INIT step (which is optional and present in ALL routes except QUICK)

**Key code:**
```python
# Step 3b: Generate ARCHITECTURE.md if it doesn't exist
arch_path = self.repo_path / "ARCHITECTURE.md"
if not arch_path.exists() and self.runner:
    logger.info("No ARCHITECTURE.md found — generating one")
    self.runner._run_claude("Run /dirigent:generate-architecture", timeout=600)
elif arch_path.exists():
    logger.info("ARCHITECTURE.md exists — skipping generation")
```

**Which routes include it:**
- **GREENFIELD** — via optional INIT step (line 104-107)
- **LEGACY** — via optional INIT step (line 146-149)
- **HYBRID** — via optional INIT step (line 187-190)
- **TESTABILITY** — via REQUIRED INIT step (line 229-231)
- **TRACKING** — via optional INIT step (line 269-272)

**Important:** ARCHITECTURE.md is generated automatically in the INIT phase (`init_phase.run()`) and is **NOT part of any specific route step sequence**. It's generated unconditionally if it doesn't exist, regardless of which route is chosen. The skill invoked is `/dirigent:generate-architecture`.

---

## 3. TEST-HARNESS GENERATION

    )

```

**Which routes include it:**
- **GREENFIELD** — via optional INIT step
- **LEGACY** — via optional INIT step

- **TRACKING** — via optional INIT step

**Important:** test-harness.json is generated during the INIT phase by the `/dirigent:run-init` skill, which:
1. Tries to run `.outbid/init.sh` or `init.sh` if it exists
2. If the init script produces test-harness.json directly, uses that
3. Otherwise, invokes the `/dirigent:run-init` skill to inspect the repo and build a best-effort harness

---

## 4. ROUTE DECISION LOGIC

**Location:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/analyzer.py`, lines 850-964

### **Primary routing: LLM-based** (lines 850-874)
First tries to use Claude via `/outbid_dirigent/llm_router.py` to decide the route. The LLM gets:
- Full spec content
- Commit count
- Test harness summary (if available)
- System prompt with route descriptions

### **Fallback routing: Heuristic** (lines 876-964)
If LLM routing fails, uses heuristic signals:

**Legacy signals (require ≥3-4 signals):**
- Repo inactive for >1 year (+2 signals)
- Language migration (e.g., Java→Python) (+3 signals)
- Legacy keywords in spec ("migrate", "refactor", etc.) (+1-2 signals)
- Large project (>2000 commits) (+1 signal)

**Greenfield signals (require ≥2-3 signals):**
- Greenfield keywords ("build", "create", "new", etc.) (+2 signals)
- Modern language (TypeScript, JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust) (+1 signal)
- Active project (<90 days since last commit) (+1 signal)
- Small project (<50 files) (+1 signal)

**Route selection:**
- `legacy` if legacy_signals ≥ 3-4
- `greenfield` if greenfield_signals ≥ 2-3
- `hybrid` as default

**File locations:**
- Route decision saved to `.dirigent/ROUTE.json` (line 380)
- LLM route decision saved to `.dirigent/LLM_ROUTE.json` (llm_router.py, line 152)
- Analysis result saved to `.dirigent/analysis.json` (analyzer.py, line 967+)

## 5. EXECUTION FLOW

The route is executed in `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/dirigent.py`, lines 345-492 in the `run_execution()` function:

For each step in `route.steps`:

   - `StepType.SHIP` → `executor.ship()` (line 477)
3. Mark step complete in STATE.json (line 484)
4. If step fails and is required, abort; if optional, continue

The actual execution methods are in `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/executor.py`.

content.text:
Klare Antwort: **Ja, ARCHITECTURE.md wird auch in der Legacy-Route geschrieben** — und zwar im selben INIT-Schritt wie bei Greenfield.

## Das Problem in deinem Fall

Die ARCHITECTURE.md und test-harness werden in `init_phase.py:569-587` generiert, **bevor die Route überhaupt feststeht**. Der INIT-Schritt ist bei allen Routen identisch:

1. `test-harness.json` generieren (Zeile 569-576)
2. `ARCHITECTURE.md` generieren falls nicht vorhanden (Zeile 581-587)

Beide werden von einem LLM-Agent geschrieben (`/dirigent:run-init` und `/dirigent:generate-architecture`). Wenn der Agent den Login-Endpunkt falsch beschreibt, fließt das in **alle** Downstream-Schritte:

- **Greenfield**: PLANNING liest ARCHITECTURE.md → falscher Plan → falsche Execution
- **Legacy**: BUSINESS_RULE_EXTRACTION liest auch den Code direkt, aber PLANNING liest trotzdem ARCHITECTURE.md → gleicher Fehler möglich
- **Hybrid**: QUICK_SCAN + PLANNING → gleicher Fehler

## Unterschied Legacy vs Greenfield

Der Legacy-Pfad hat einen **zusätzlichen** Schritt `BUSINESS_RULE_EXTRACTION`, der Regeln direkt aus dem Code extrahiert. Das hätte den Fehler **abmildern** können, weil der Planner die extrahierten Business Rules gegen die falsche ARCHITECTURE.md hätte abgleichen können. Aber es gibt keine Validierung, die Widersprüche zwischen ARCHITECTURE.md und BUSINESS_RULES.md erkennt.

## Kernproblem

Die ARCHITECTURE.md wird einmal vom LLM geschrieben und dann als Ground Truth behandelt — ohne Verifikation. Wenn sie falsch ist, propagiert der Fehler durch die ganze Pipeline.

Soll ich das als konkretes Issue angehen — z.B. einen Verification-Step nach der ARCHITECTURE.md-Generierung, der die dokumentierten Endpoints gegen tatsächliche Routen im Code abgleicht?

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/jk/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.7/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Offer visual companion** (if topic will involve visual questions) — this is its own message, not combined with a clarifying question. See the Visual Companion section below.
3. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
4. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
5. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
6. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
7. **Spec self-review** — quick inline check for placeholders, contradictions, ambiguity, scope (see below)
8. **User reviews written spec** — ask user to review the spec file before proceeding
9. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Visual questions ahead?" [shape=diamond];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" [shape=box];
    "User reviews spec?" [shape=diamond];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Visual questions ahead?";
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" [label="yes"];
    "Visual questions ahead?" -> "Ask clarifying questions" [label="no"];
    "Offer Visual Companion\n(own message, no other content)" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)";
    "Spec self-review\n(fix inline)" -> "User reviews spec?";
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Write design doc" [label="changes requested"];
    "User reviews spec?" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill" [label="approved"];
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**

- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Before asking detailed questions, assess scope: if the request describes multiple independent subsystems (e.g., "build a platform with chat, file storage, billing, and analytics"), flag this immediately. Don't spend questions refining details of a project that needs to be decomposed first.
- If the project is too large for a single spec, help the user decompose into sub-projects: what are the independent pieces, how do they relate, what order should they be built? Then brainstorm the first sub-project through the normal design flow. Each sub-project gets its own spec → plan → implementation cycle.
- For appropriately-scoped projects, ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**

- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**

- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

**Design for isolation and clarity:**

- Break the system into smaller units that each have one clear purpose, communicate through well-defined interfaces, and can be understood and tested independently
- For each unit, you should be able to answer: what does it do, how do you use it, and what does it depend on?
- Can someone understand what a unit does without reading its internals? Can you change the internals without breaking consumers? If not, the boundaries need work.
- Smaller, well-bounded units are also easier for you to work with - you reason better about code you can hold in context at once, and your edits are more reliable when files are focused. When a file grows large, that's often a signal that it's doing too much.

**Working in existing codebases:**

- Explore the current structure before proposing changes. Follow existing patterns.
- Where existing code has problems that affect the work (e.g., a file that's grown too large, unclear boundaries, tangled responsibilities), include targeted improvements as part of the design - the way a good developer improves code they're working in.
- Don't propose unrelated refactoring. Stay focused on what serves the current goal.

## After the Design

**Documentation:**

- Write the validated design (spec) to `docs/superpowers/specs/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
  - (User preferences for spec location override this default)
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Spec Self-Review:**
After writing the spec document, look at it with fresh eyes:

1. **Placeholder scan:** Any "TBD", "TODO", incomplete sections, or vague requirements? Fix them.
2. **Internal consistency:** Do any sections contradict each other? Does the architecture match the feature descriptions?
3. **Scope check:** Is this focused enough for a single implementation plan, or does it need decomposition?
4. **Ambiguity check:** Could any requirement be interpreted two different ways? If so, pick one and make it explicit.

Fix any issues inline. No need to re-review — just fix and move on.

**User Review Gate:**
After the spec review loop passes, ask the user to review the written spec before proceeding:

> "Spec written and committed to `<path>`. Please review it and let me know if you want to make any changes before we start writing out the implementation plan."

Wait for the user's response. If they request changes, make them and re-run the spec review loop. Only proceed once the user approves.

**Implementation:**

- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

## Visual Companion

A browser-based companion for showing mockups, diagrams, and visual options during brainstorming. Available as a tool — not a mode. Accepting the companion means it's available for questions that benefit from visual treatment; it does NOT mean every question goes through the browser.

**Offering the companion:** When you anticipate that upcoming questions will involve visual content (mockups, layouts, diagrams), offer it once for consent:
> "Some of what we're working on might be easier to explain if I can show it to you in a web browser. I can put together mockups, diagrams, comparisons, and other visuals as we go. This feature is still new and can be token-intensive. Want to try it? (Requires opening a local URL)"

**This offer MUST be its own message.** Do not combine it with clarifying questions, context summaries, or any other content. The message should contain ONLY the offer above and nothing else. Wait for the user's response before continuing. If they decline, proceed with text-only brainstorming.

**Per-question decision:** Even after the user accepts, decide FOR EACH QUESTION whether to use the browser or the terminal. The test: **would the user understand this better by seeing it than reading it?**

- **Use the browser** for content that IS visual — mockups, wireframes, layout comparisons, architecture diagrams, side-by-side visual designs
- **Use the terminal** for content that is text — requirements questions, conceptual choices, tradeoff lists, A/B/C/D text options, scope decisions

A question about a UI topic is not automatically a visual question. "What does personality mean in this context?" is a conceptual question — use the terminal. "Which wizard layout works better?" is a visual question — use the browser.

If they agree to the companion, read the detailed guide before proceeding:
`skills/brainstorming/visual-companion.md`

input.prompt:
In the outbid-dirigent project, I need to understand how ARCHITECTURE.md and test-harness.json are consumed DOWNSTREAM after being generated in init_phase.py.

Specifically:
1. Which skills/agents/prompts reference ARCHITECTURE.md? Grep for "ARCHITECTURE" across all .md, .py files
2. Which skills/agents/prompts reference test-harness? Grep for "test-harness" and "test_harness" across all files
3. How does the planner (create_plan / planning step) use these files? Does it inject them into the prompt?
4. How does the executor use them?
5. How does the contract negotiator use them?

I want to understand the full "blast radius" — everywhere a wrong ARCHITECTURE.md or test-harness.json would cause downstream damage. Report file paths and line numbers.

In the outbid-dirigent project, find and read the /dirigent:generate-architecture skill. It's likely in src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/generate-architecture/SKILL.md or similar.

I need to understand:
1. What instructions does the LLM get for generating ARCHITECTURE.md?
2. What sources does it read from? (code, config files, etc.)
3. Does it run any verification commands or just read files?
4. What's the expected format/structure of the output?

Also find and read the /dirigent:run-init skill to understand how test-harness.json is generated.

Report full content of both skills with file paths.

content.text:
Perfect! I have both skill files. Here's the complete information you requested:

## File Locations

1. **Generate Architecture Skill**: `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/generate-architecture/SKILL.md`
2. **Run-Init Skill**: `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/run-init/SKILL.md`

---

## Summary of `/dirigent:generate-architecture`

### 1. LLM Instructions for Generating ARCHITECTURE.md

The skill instructs the agent (infra-architect) to produce an agent-readable map of the codebase with the following philosophy:
- **Purpose**: Eliminate the 10-15 minute cold-start penalty by giving any agent landing in the repo everything needed to navigate and modify it confidently
- **Philosophy**: Every section exists because an agent will need it to make better decisions (not documentation for documentation's sake)
- **Modes**: Default (create from scratch) or `--update` (verify and update existing sections)

### 2. Sources Read

The skill performs strategic code surveys:
- **Package managers**: `package.json`, `pyproject.toml`, `Cargo.toml`, `go.mod`, `pom.xml`, `build.gradle`, `Gemfile`, `composer.json`
- **Framework imports**: Grep across Python/TypeScript/JavaScript files for specific framework imports (FastAPI, Django, Flask, Express, Next, Nuxt, Nest)
- **Module structure**: Find all `.py` files, `.ts`/`.tsx` files, monorepo structures
- **Entry points**: Main functions, CLI scripts, route definitions, worker/queue consumers
- **Configuration**: Env vars, config loading code, `.env.example`
- **Dependencies**: Direct inspection of actual imports, not just dependency lists
- **Validation**: Grep to verify file paths exist, check versions against package managers

### 3. Verification Commands

The skill performs **read-only verification** (no execution):
- Uses `ls`, `grep`, and file reading to verify every claim
- Checks file paths with `ls` or glob before including them
- Tests Mermaid diagram syntax by reading them back
- Cross-checks env vars with actual config loading code
- No bash execution for testing — purely static code analysis

### 4. Expected Format/Structure of Output

Creates `ARCHITECTURE.md` at repo root with 10 required sections:

1. **System Overview** - Mermaid diagram (5-10 boxes max) showing high-level components
2. **Tech Stack** - Table with Language, Framework, Database, Cache, Auth, Testing, Deployment, Package Manager
3. **Directory Structure** - Top 2 levels of meaningful directories with purpose annotations
4. **Entry Points** - For each: what triggers it, where code lives, what it does
5. **Module Architecture** - Mermaid diagram showing internal module dependencies (layered, pointing downward)
6. **Data Model** - Mermaid ERD or class diagram with 5-10 most important entities only
7. **Key Patterns** - 3-5 recurring patterns with concrete examples and usage guidance
8. **API Surface** - Route groups/public API functions organized in tables with purpose and auth level
9. **Configuration** - Table of env vars, their purpose, and required status
10. **External Dependencies** - Services with purpose and failure modes
11. **Development Workflow** - Exact setup, run, and test commands

**Special notes**:
- Preserve sections marked with `<!-- manual -->...<!-- /manual -->` in update mode
- Never commit if standalone (let user review); always commit if in Dirigent pipeline
- Max 15 minutes to complete; prioritize structure over exhaustive listing

---

## Summary of `/dirigent:run-init`

### 1. Purpose

Generates a test harness specification (`test-harness.json`) that tells reviewers how to verify features end-to-end.

### 2. Sources Read

The skill inspects for:
- **Tech stack & dev server**: `package.json` scripts, `docker-compose.yml`, `.env` files, framework config (`vite.config`, `next.config`)
- **Authentication**: `/api/auth` routes, NextAuth/Clerk/Supabase config, Playwright `globalSetup` files, `storageState` references, seed scripts
- **Seed data**: `package.json` seed scripts, `prisma/seed.ts`, `fixtures/` directory, SQL seed files, test setup files
- **Health checks**: `/api/health`, `/health` endpoints, database checks (`pg_isready`), docker-compose health
- **E2E frameworks**: `playwright.config.ts`, `cypress.config.ts`, custom Puppeteer scripts
- **Port/Base URL**: `.env`/`.env.local`, package.json "dev" script flags, Vite/Next config, Docker mappings

### 3. Verification Commands

**Only reads and analyzes** — no execution. The skill discovers:
- Commands that could be run (but doesn't run them)
- Concrete curl commands and health checks the reviewer can use
- e2e test run commands

### 4. Expected Format/Structure of Output

Outputs valid JSON file at `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json` with this schema:

```json
{
  "base_url": "http://localhost:3000",
  "port": 3000,
  "auth": {
    "method": "bearer_token | cookie | storage_state | api_key | basic_auth | none",
    "token_env_var": "env var name (never actual token)",
    "login_command": "curl command that returns token/session",
    "storage_state_path": "path to Playwright storageState.json if applicable",
    "username_env_var": "",
    "password_env_var": ""
  },
  "seed": {
    "users": [{ "role": "", "email": "", "password_env_var": "", "password_default": "", "notes": "" }],
    "description": "What data exists after seeding",
    "seed_command": "Command to re-run seeding"
  },
  "health_checks": [
    { "name": "string", "command": "bash command", "timeout_seconds": 30 }
  ],
  "e2e_framework": {
    "framework": "playwright | puppeteer | cypress | none",
    "config_file": "path to config",
    "run_command": "command to run tests",
    "has_global_setup": boolean,
    "global_setup_file": ""
  },
  "verification_commands": [
    { "name": "What this verifies", "command": "runnable bash command", "expected": "success criteria" }
  ],
  "services": ["postgres", "redis"],
  "testability_score": 0-10,
  "testability_rationale": "Why this score",
  "testability_description": "Plain text description of test setup",
  "testability_gaps": ["actionable improvements"],
  "status": "ready",
  "notes": ""
}
```

**Testability scoring rubric**:
- **0-2** (Untestable): No dev config, no framework, no seed data
- **3-4** (Minimal): Dev server runs, but no auth/seed/e2e
- **5-6** (Partial): Dev server + some seed OR auth, but gaps remain
- **7-8** (Good): Dev server + auth + seed + e2e framework, minor gaps
- **9-10** (Excellent): Full stack, auth works, rich seed data, e2e passes, health checks green

**Important constraints**:
- NEVER include actual passwords/tokens — only env var names
- Every verification command must be concrete, runnable bash
- Health check commands must exit 0 when healthy, non-zero when not
- Validate output against schema using `validate_schema.py` before completing
- Output ONLY the JSON file — no markdown or commentary

content.text:
Excellent. Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me create a final summary of all the findings:

## Summary Report: ARCHITECTURE.md and test-harness.json Consumption

### Overview

I've traced the complete flow of both `ARCHITECTURE.md` and `test-harness.json` from generation through downstream consumption. Here's the full "blast radius" analysis:

---

### 1. **ARCHITECTURE.md References**

#### Generation (Init Phase)
- **Location:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py` (lines 581-587)
  - Generated by `/dirigent:generate-architecture` skill if not present
  - Stored at repo root: `ARCHITECTURE.md`

#### Consumption Points

**A. Spec Generation Context** (`dirigent.py` lines 98-113)
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/dirigent.py` (lines 102, 108-111)
- **Purpose:** When generating a spec from user description, `ARCHITECTURE.md` is read and included as context
- **Impact:** Used to provide baseline understanding of the system when writing specs

**B. Create-Plan Skill** (`create-plan/SKILL.md` lines 29-30, 86)
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/create-plan/SKILL.md`
- **Lines:** 29, 86
- **Purpose:** Planner reads ARCHITECTURE.md to understand system structure when creating task plans
- **Impact:** Task descriptions and phase organization reflect architectural patterns
- **Consumer:** `infra-architect` agent
- **Action:** Read in "Gather context first" step before asking clarifying questions

**C. Contract Negotiator (via Planner)**
- **Indirect reference:** Contract negotiator reads files gathered by planner, which include ARCHITECTURE context
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/agents/contract-negotiator.md`
- **Context:** Acceptance criteria should be shaped by the architecture

---

### 2. **test-harness.json References**

#### Generation (Init Phase)
- **Location:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py` (lines 436-441, 567-577)
- **Path:** `.dirigent/test-harness.json`
- **Generated by:** `/dirigent:run-init` skill if init script doesn't produce it
- **Schema defined:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/test_harness_schema.py` (lines 88-156)

#### Consumption Points

**A. Planner (create-plan skill)** 
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/create-plan/SKILL.md` (line 85)
- **Lines:** 85, 149
- **Path:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json`
- **Purpose:** Planner reads to understand test infrastructure for creating verification tasks
- **Impact:** Task descriptions reference verification_commands and e2e_framework from harness

**B. Contract Negotiator (create-contract)** 
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/agents/contract-negotiator.md` (line 19)
- **Lines:** 19
- **Purpose:** Probes test infrastructure (base_url, auth, seed data, health checks) to write executable verification commands
- **Action:** Before writing verification commands, probes base_url and test runner availability
- **Impact:** Acceptance criteria must be executable against actual test harness infrastructure

**C. Reviewer (review-phase skill)**
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/agents/reviewer.md` (line 18)
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/review-phase/SKILL.md` (lines 5, 22, 25-26, 62-96)
- **Lines:** 18 (agent), 5, 22, 25-26, 62-96 (skill)
- **Path:** `${DIRIGENT_RUN_DIR}/test-harness.json`
- **Purpose:** MANDATORY e2e verification — runs health checks, login commands, and verification_commands
- **Action:** 
  - Line 22: Reads test-harness.json
  - Line 25-26: Runs health checks, login flow, verification commands
  - Line 62-96: Quality checklist includes "E2e-Verifikation: ALLE verification_commands ausfuehren"
- **Impact:** Review verdict depends entirely on test-harness verification commands working

**D. Executor (run_tests method)**
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/executor.py` (lines 637-669)
- **Lines:** 639, 644, 648, 651-653
- **Purpose:** Final verification step — executor runs all test harness verification commands
- **Action:** 
  - Line 639: Loads test harness
  - Line 644: Iterates through verification_commands
  - Line 651-653: Runs e2e framework if configured
- **Impact:** Test failure blocks ship

**E. Increase-Testability Skill**
- **File:** `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/plugin/skills/increase-testability/SKILL.md` (line 11)
- **Lines:** 11
- **Purpose:** Analyzes current testability score and gaps from harness
- **Impact:** Recommends improvements to harness infrastructure

---

### 3. **Data Flow Diagram**

```
init_phase.py (lines 581-587)
  └─> /dirigent:generate-architecture
        └─> ARCHITECTURE.md (repo root)
             └─> Consumed by:
                 ├─> dirigent.py line 102 (spec generation context)
                 └─> /dirigent:create-plan lines 29, 86
                      └─> Task descriptions reflect arch patterns

init_phase.py (lines 436-441, 567-577)
  └─> /dirigent:run-init
        └─> test-harness.json (.dirigent/)
             └─> Consumed by:
                 ├─> /dirigent:create-plan line 85
                 │    └─> Tasks reference verification_commands
                 ├─> /dirigent:create-contract line 19
                 │    └─> Contract verifications must match harness
                 ├─> /dirigent:review-phase lines 22, 25-26
                 │    └─> MANDATORY e2e verification
                 ├─> executor.py lines 639-653
                 │    └─> Final test step before ship
                 └─> /dirigent:increase-testability line 11
                      └─> Analyzes gaps
```

---

### 4. **Blast Radius Analysis**

#### If ARCHITECTURE.md is WRONG or INCOMPLETE:

1. **Spec Generation** — Context-gathering phase gets bad foundation (line 102 in dirigent.py)
2. **Plan Tasks** — Task descriptions may violate actual architecture, causing executor to take wrong approach (lines 29, 86 in create-plan SKILL.md)
3. **Acceptance Criteria** — Criteria may not match how system actually works
4. **Code Review** — Reviewer might not catch architectural violations if they don't have correct reference

#### If test-harness.json is WRONG or INCOMPLETE:

1. **Plan Creation FAILS** — Planner can't determine verification strategy (line 85 in create-plan SKILL.md)
2. **Contract Verification FAILS** — Contract negotiator can't write executable verification commands (line 19 in contract-negotiator.md)
3. **Code Review FAILS** — Reviewer can't run e2e tests, all criteria marked as `"warn"` (lines 22, 25-26 in review-phase SKILL.md)
4. **Ship is BLOCKED** — Executor fails verification commands (lines 639-653 in executor.py)
5. **Full Phase Iteration Loop** — Reviewer marks criteria as "warn" due to infra failure, phase is marked partial/failed, executor has to re-run

---

### 5. **Critical Dependencies**

| File | Generation | Consumers | Failure Mode |
|------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| ARCHITECTURE.md | init_phase.py line 581-587 | create-plan skill, dirigent spec generation | Weak task descriptions, wrong patterns |
| test-harness.json | init_phase.py line 567-577 | create-plan, contract-negotiator, reviewer, executor | Review fails, ship blocked, iteration loop |

---

### 6. **Line-by-Line Reference Map**

**ARCHITECTURE.md:**
- Generated: `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/init_phase.py:581-587`
- Consumed: `/Users/jk/projects/outbid-dirigent/src/outbid_dirigent/dirigent.py:102` (context gathering)

**test-harness.json:**

content.text:
Hier ist was ich aus der Exploration gelernt habe:

**Blast Radius einer falschen ARCHITECTURE.md / test-harness.json:**
- ARCHITECTURE.md fließt in: Spec-Generierung, create-plan, und indirekt in Contract-Negotiation
- test-harness.json fließt in: create-plan, contract-negotiator, reviewer, executor run_tests — also **überall**
- Beide werden rein durch statische Code-Analyse generiert (kein Ausführen, kein Testen) — der LLM liest Dateien und rät

Das Kernproblem: Beide Skills lesen nur Code und inferieren daraus Endpoints/Auth/Config. Kein einziger `curl` oder Testlauf verifiziert die Behauptungen.

questions.question: Was war in deinem konkreten Fall falsch — hat der LLM den Login-Endpunkt falsch aus dem Code gelesen (z.B. /api/auth/login statt /auth/callback), oder war der Endpunkt korrekt beschrieben aber zur Laufzeit anders (weil Middleware/Redirects)?

options.label: Laufzeit-Divergenz
options.description: Der Code beschreibt es korrekt, aber zur Laufzeit verhält es sich anders (Middleware, Redirects, ENV-abhängig)

options.label: Beides / Unsicher
options.description: Nicht mehr genau nachvollziehbar

content.text:
OK. Zusammenfassung der Anforderungen:

- **Problem**: LLM schreibt ARCHITECTURE.md / test-harness.json falsch, Fehler propagiert durch ganze Pipeline
- **LLM-Verification ist raus** — genauso fehleranfällig
- **Ziel**: Nachvollziehbarkeit — jede Behauptung muss eine Quellenangabe haben, damit man (oder ein Downstream-Agent) nachprüfen kann

Hier sind **3 Ansätze**:

### Ansatz A: "Cited Architecture" — Quellenangaben in ARCHITECTURE.md

Jede Behauptung in ARCHITECTURE.md bekommt eine Quellenangabe im Format `[source: path/to/file.ts:42]`. Der generate-architecture Skill wird angewiesen, für jeden Endpoint, jede Route, jede Config-Behauptung die konkrete Datei+Zeile anzugeben wo er es gefunden hat.

- **Pro**: Minimal invasiv, keine Pipeline-Änderung, sofort debuggbar
- **Contra**: Quellenangaben können auch falsch sein (LLM halluziniert Zeilennummer). Downstream-Agents müssen die Quellen nicht zwingend prüfen.

### Ansatz B: "Evidence-First Generation" — Generierung mit strukturiertem Beweismaterial

Der generate-architecture und run-init Skill werden umgebaut: Statt "lies den Code und schreib eine Zusammenfassung" wird es zu "führe diese Grep/Glob-Befehle aus, sammle die Ergebnisse, und synthetisiere daraus". Die Beweiskette (welcher Befehl → welches Ergebnis → welche Behauptung) wird als Sidecar-Datei gespeichert.

- **Pro**: Behauptungen sind mechanisch nachprüfbar, nicht nur "der LLM sagt Zeile 42"
- **Contra**: Aufwändiger, Skill-Rewrite nötig, Sidecar-Datei muss gemanaged werden

### Ansatz C: "Lazy Architecture" — ARCHITECTURE.md nicht vorab generieren

ARCHITECTURE.md wird gar nicht im INIT generiert. Stattdessen liest jeder Downstream-Step (Planner, Contract-Negotiator, Reviewer) den Code selbst. ARCHITECTURE.md wird optional als Cache behandelt, nie als Ground Truth.

- **Pro**: Eliminiert das Problem komplett — kein Single Point of Failure
- **Contra**: Jeder Step braucht mehr Tokens/Zeit. Planner muss selbst Code lesen. Redundante Arbeit.

---

**Meine Empfehlung: Ansatz A**, weil er dein konkretes Problem löst (Nachvollziehbarkeit) mit minimalen Änderungen. Die Quellenangaben machen ARCHITECTURE.md von einem Black-Box-Dokument zu einem auditierbaren Artefakt. Wenn eine Quellenangabe falsch ist, sieht man es sofort.

Ansatz B wäre die robustere Variante, aber deutlich mehr Aufwand. Ansatz C ist radikal aber bricht das aktuelle Design.

2	name: generate-architecture
3	description: Generate ARCHITECTURE.md for the target repository — a complete, agent-readable map of the codebase
4	context: fork
5	agent: infra-architect
6	---
7	
8	# Generate Architecture
9	
10	You produce `ARCHITECTURE.md` — a single file that gives any agent (or human) landing in this repo everything it needs to navigate and modify the codebase confidently.
11	
12	## Why This Matters
13	
14	Without an architecture doc, every agent session starts with 10-15 minutes of codebase exploration. Worse, agents make wrong assumptions about how modules connect, where data flows, and what patterns to follow. ARCHITECTURE.md eliminates this cold-start penalty.
15	
16	This is NOT documentation for documentation's sake. Every section exists because an agent will need it to make better decisions.
17	
18	## Mode
19	
20	Check `$ARGUMENTS` for mode:
21	- **Default (generate)**: Create ARCHITECTURE.md from scratch
22	- **`--update`**: Read existing ARCHITECTURE.md, verify each section against current code, update what changed, preserve manually-written sections marked with `<!-- manual -->`
23	
24	## Step 1: Survey the Codebase
25	
26	Before writing anything, build a mental model:
27	
28	### 1a. Identify the tech stack
29	```bash
30	# Package managers and dependencies
31	ls package.json pyproject.toml Cargo.toml go.mod pom.xml build.gradle Gemfile composer.json 2>/dev/null
32	
33	# Frameworks — check actual imports, not just deps
34	grep -r "from fastapi\|from django\|from flask\|from express\|from next\|from nuxt\|from nest" --include="*.py" --include="*.ts" --include="*.js" -l | head -20
35	```
36	
37	### 1b. Identify entry points
38	Every codebase has entry points — where execution starts. Find them:
39	- **CLI**: `main()`, `if __name__ == "__main__"`, `bin/` scripts, package.json `bin` field
40	- **Web**: route definitions, `app.py`, `server.ts`, `pages/`, `app/` directories
41	- **Workers**: queue consumers, cron definitions, Celery tasks, background jobs
42	- **Libraries**: public API surface, `__init__.py` exports, `index.ts` re-exports
43	
44	### 1c. Map the module structure
45	```bash
46	# For Python
47	find . -name "*.py" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -not -path "*/.venv/*" | head -50
48	
49	# For TypeScript/JavaScript
50	find . -name "*.ts" -o -name "*.tsx" | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .next | head -50
51	
52	# For monorepos
53	ls -d packages/*/package.json apps/*/package.json 2>/dev/null
54	```
55	
56	### 1d. Identify data flow
57	- Where does data enter the system? (API endpoints, CLI args, message queues, file uploads)
58	- Where is data stored? (databases, caches, file system, external services)
59	- How does data move between modules? (function calls, events, queues, HTTP)
60	
61	### 1e. Identify key patterns
62	Look for recurring patterns the codebase uses:
63	- Repository/service pattern
64	- Event-driven architecture
65	- Middleware chains
66	- Plugin/hook systems
67	- State machines
68	- Queue-based processing
69	
70	## Step 2: Write ARCHITECTURE.md
71	
72	Use this exact structure. Every section is required. If a section doesn't apply, include it with a note explaining why.
73	
74	```markdown
75	# {Project Name} — Architecture
76	
77	> {One sentence: what this system does and for whom}
78	
79	## System Overview
80	
81	{Mermaid diagram showing the high-level components and how they connect.
82	For web apps: client → API → services → database.
83	For CLI tools: input → processing pipeline → output.
84	For libraries: public API → internal modules.
85	Keep it to 5-10 boxes max — this is the 30-second orientation.}
86	
87	## Tech Stack
88	
89	| Layer | Technology | Notes |
90	|-------|-----------|-------|
91	| Language | {e.g. TypeScript 5.x} | |
92	| Framework | {e.g. Next.js 14 (App Router)} | |
93	| Database | {e.g. PostgreSQL via Prisma} | |
94	| Cache | {e.g. Redis} | {if applicable} |
95	| Auth | {e.g. NextAuth.js / Clerk / custom JWT} | |
96	| Testing | {e.g. Vitest + Playwright} | |
97	| Deployment | {e.g. Vercel / Docker / AWS} | |
98	| Package Manager | {e.g. pnpm / uv / cargo} | |
99	
100	## Directory Structure
101	
102	{Show the top 2 levels of meaningful directories. Annotate each with its purpose.
103	Skip node_modules, .git, __pycache__, etc.}
104	
105	```
106	project/
107	├── src/               # Application source
108	│   ├── app/           # Next.js App Router pages
109	│   ├── components/    # React components
110	│   ├── lib/           # Shared utilities and clients
111	│   └── server/        # Server-side logic (API routes, services)
112	├── prisma/            # Database schema and migrations
113	├── tests/             # Test suites
114	└── scripts/           # Developer tooling
115	```
116	
117	## Entry Points
118	
119	{List every way execution enters the system. For each, state:
120	- What triggers it (HTTP request, CLI command, cron, queue message)
121	- Where the code lives (file path)
122	- What it does in one sentence}
123	
124	## Module Architecture
125	
126	{Mermaid diagram showing how internal modules depend on each other.
127	Group by layer (API/Routes → Services/Business Logic → Data/Infrastructure).
128	Show the direction of dependencies — dependencies should point downward.}
129	
130	## Data Model
131	
132	{If the project has a database, show the key entities and their relationships.
133	Use a mermaid ERD or class diagram. Only include the 5-10 most important entities.
134	For each entity, list the 3-5 most important fields, not every column.}
135	
136	## Key Patterns
137	
138	{Describe 3-5 patterns that recur throughout the codebase. For each:
139	- Name the pattern
140	- Show one concrete example (file path + brief code reference)
141	- Explain when to use it (so the agent follows the pattern for new code)}
142	
143	Example patterns:
144	- "All API routes use the `withAuth` middleware for authentication"
145	- "Database queries go through repository classes, never direct Prisma calls in routes"
146	- "Background jobs extend the `BaseJob` class and are registered in `jobs/index.ts`"
147	
148	## API Surface
149	
150	{For web apps: list the main API route groups with their purpose.
151	For libraries: list the public API functions/classes.
152	For CLI tools: list the commands and subcommands.
153	Don't list every endpoint — group them.}
154	
155	| Route Group | Purpose | Auth |
156	|-------------|---------|------|
157	| `/api/auth/*` | Authentication (login, register, session) | Public |
158	| `/api/users/*` | User management | Admin |
159	| `/api/projects/*` | Project CRUD and collaboration | User |
160	
161	## Configuration
162	
163	{Where config lives, what env vars are required, and how config flows into the app.}
164	
165	| Variable | Purpose | Required |
166	|----------|---------|----------|
167	| `DATABASE_URL` | PostgreSQL connection string | Yes |
168	| `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` | Session encryption key | Yes |
169	
170	## External Dependencies
171	
172	{Services this system talks to. For each: what, why, and what happens if it's down.}
173	
174	| Service | Purpose | Failure Mode |
175	|---------|---------|-------------|
176	| PostgreSQL | Primary data store | App down |
177	| Redis | Session cache, rate limiting | Degraded (falls back to DB) |
178	| Stripe | Payment processing | Payments fail, app continues |
179	
180	## Development Workflow
181	
182	{How to go from a fresh clone to a running system. Be specific — exact commands.}
183	
184	```bash
185	# Setup
186	{exact commands}
187	
188	# Run
189	{exact commands}
190	
191	# Test
192	{exact commands}
193	```
194	```
195	
196	## Step 3: Validate
197	
198	After writing, verify:
199	
200	1. **Every file path mentioned exists** — grep for each path you cited
201	2. **Every module/class/function mentioned exists** — they may have been renamed
202	3. **Mermaid diagrams render** — check syntax is valid
203	4. **Tech stack matches reality** — versions match package.json/pyproject.toml
204	5. **Entry points are complete** — no missing route files, CLI commands, or workers
205	6. **Env vars match** — cross-check with .env.example or actual config loading code
206	
207	## Step 4: Commit (if in Dirigent pipeline)
208	
209	If running as part of the Dirigent pipeline:
210	```bash
211	git add ARCHITECTURE.md && git commit -m "docs: generate ARCHITECTURE.md"
212	```
213	
214	If running standalone (user invoked directly), do NOT commit — let the user review first.
215	
216	## Update Mode (--update)
217	
218	When `--update` is passed:
219	
220	1. Read the existing ARCHITECTURE.md
221	2. For each section, verify claims against current code
222	3. Update sections that are stale (wrong file paths, missing modules, changed patterns)
223	4. Preserve sections wrapped in `<!-- manual -->...<!-- /manual -->` — these were hand-written
224	5. Add new sections for things that didn't exist when the doc was first written
225	6. Remove sections for things that no longer exist
226	7. Commit: `git add ARCHITECTURE.md && git commit -m "docs: update ARCHITECTURE.md"`
227	
228	## Rules
229	
230	<rules>
231	<rule>Every claim must be verified against the actual code — never guess or assume</rule>
232	<rule>File paths must be checked with ls or glob before writing them into the doc</rule>
233	<rule>Prefer showing 5 important things over listing 50 things exhaustively</rule>
234	<rule>Mermaid diagrams must use valid syntax — test by reading them back</rule>
235	<rule>The doc must be useful on day 1 to an agent with zero context — no jargon without explanation</rule>
236	<rule>If running as part of Dirigent init, always commit. If standalone, never commit.</rule>
237	<rule>Do not include implementation details that change frequently — focus on structure and patterns</rule>
238	<rule>For monorepos, show the package/app structure and how packages depend on each other</rule>
239	</rules>
240

242	<constraint>Output is ARCHITECTURE.md at the repo root — one file only</constraint>
243	<constraint>Maximum 15 minutes — prioritize structure and patterns over exhaustive listing</constraint>
244	<constraint>Do not read every file — sample strategically (entry points, config, key modules)</constraint>

---
name: generate-architecture
description: Generate ARCHITECTURE.md for the target repository — a complete, agent-readable map of the codebase
context: fork
agent: infra-architect
---

# Generate Architecture

You produce `ARCHITECTURE.md` — a single file that gives any agent (or human) landing in this repo everything it needs to navigate and modify the codebase confidently.

## Why This Matters

Without an architecture doc, every agent session starts with 10-15 minutes of codebase exploration. Worse, agents make wrong assumptions about how modules connect, where data flows, and what patterns to follow. ARCHITECTURE.md eliminates this cold-start penalty.

This is NOT documentation for documentation's sake. Every section exists because an agent will need it to make better decisions.

## Mode

Check `$ARGUMENTS` for mode:
- **Default (generate)**: Create ARCHITECTURE.md from scratch
- **`--update`**: Read existing ARCHITECTURE.md, verify each section against current code, update what changed, preserve manually-written sections marked with `<!-- manual -->`

## Step 1: Survey the Codebase

Before writing anything, build a mental model:

### 1a. Identify the tech stack
```bash
# Package managers and dependencies
ls package.json pyproject.toml Cargo.toml go.mod pom.xml build.gradle Gemfile composer.json 2>/dev/null

# Frameworks — check actual imports, not just deps
grep -r "from fastapi\|from django\|from flask\|from express\|from next\|from nuxt\|from nest" --include="*.py" --include="*.ts" --include="*.js" -l | head -20
```

### 1b. Identify entry points
Every codebase has entry points — where execution starts. Find them:
- **CLI**: `main()`, `if __name__ == "__main__"`, `bin/` scripts, package.json `bin` field
- **Web**: route definitions, `app.py`, `server.ts`, `pages/`, `app/` directories
- **Workers**: queue consumers, cron definitions, Celery tasks, background jobs
- **Libraries**: public API surface, `__init__.py` exports, `index.ts` re-exports

### 1c. Map the module structure
```bash
# For Python
find . -name "*.py" -not -path "*/node_modules/*" -not -path "*/.venv/*" | head -50

# For TypeScript/JavaScript
find . -name "*.ts" -o -name "*.tsx" | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .next | head -50

# For monorepos
ls -d packages/*/package.json apps/*/package.json 2>/dev/null
```

### 1d. Identify data flow
- Where does data enter the system? (API endpoints, CLI args, message queues, file uploads)
- Where is data stored? (databases, caches, file system, external services)
- How does data move between modules? (function calls, events, queues, HTTP)

### 1e. Identify key patterns
Look for recurring patterns the codebase uses:
- Repository/service pattern
- Event-driven architecture
- Middleware chains
- Plugin/hook systems
- State machines
- Queue-based processing

## Step 2: Write ARCHITECTURE.md

Use this exact structure. Every section is required. If a section doesn't apply, include it with a note explaining why.

```markdown
# {Project Name} — Architecture

> {One sentence: what this system does and for whom}

## System Overview

{Mermaid diagram showing the high-level components and how they connect.
For web apps: client → API → services → database.
For CLI tools: input → processing pipeline → output.
For libraries: public API → internal modules.
Keep it to 5-10 boxes max — this is the 30-second orientation.}

## Tech Stack

| Layer | Technology | Notes |
|-------|-----------|-------|
| Language | {e.g. TypeScript 5.x} | |
| Framework | {e.g. Next.js 14 (App Router)} | |
| Database | {e.g. PostgreSQL via Prisma} | |
| Cache | {e.g. Redis} | {if applicable} |
| Auth | {e.g. NextAuth.js / Clerk / custom JWT} | |
| Testing | {e.g. Vitest + Playwright} | |
| Deployment | {e.g. Vercel / Docker / AWS} | |
| Package Manager | {e.g. pnpm / uv / cargo} | |

## Directory Structure

{Show the top 2 levels of meaningful directories. Annotate each with its purpose.
Skip node_modules, .git, __pycache__, etc.}

```
project/
├── src/               # Application source
│   ├── app/           # Next.js App Router pages
│   ├── components/    # React components
│   ├── lib/           # Shared utilities and clients
│   └── server/        # Server-side logic (API routes, services)
├── prisma/            # Database schema and migrations
├── tests/             # Test suites
└── scripts/           # Developer tooling
```

## Entry Points

{List every way execution enters the system. For each, state:
- What triggers it (HTTP request, CLI command, cron, queue message)
- Where the code lives (file path)
- What it does in one sentence}

## Module Architecture

{Mermaid diagram showing how internal modules depend on each other.
Group by layer (API/Routes → Services/Business Logic → Data/Infrastructure).
Show the direction of dependencies — dependencies should point downward.}

## Data Model

{If the project has a database, show the key entities and their relationships.
Use a mermaid ERD or class diagram. Only include the 5-10 most important entities.
For each entity, list the 3-5 most important fields, not every column.}

## Key Patterns

{Describe 3-5 patterns that recur throughout the codebase. For each:
- Name the pattern
- Show one concrete example (file path + brief code reference)
- Explain when to use it (so the agent follows the pattern for new code)}

Example patterns:
- "All API routes use the `withAuth` middleware for authentication"
- "Database queries go through repository classes, never direct Prisma calls in routes"
- "Background jobs extend the `BaseJob` class and are registered in `jobs/index.ts`"

## API Surface

{For web apps: list the main API route groups with their purpose.
For libraries: list the public API functions/classes.
For CLI tools: list the commands and subcommands.
Don't list every endpoint — group them.}

| Route Group | Purpose | Auth |
|-------------|---------|------|
| `/api/auth/*` | Authentication (login, register, session) | Public |
| `/api/users/*` | User management | Admin |
| `/api/projects/*` | Project CRUD and collaboration | User |

## Configuration

{Where config lives, what env vars are required, and how config flows into the app.}

| Variable | Purpose | Required |
|----------|---------|----------|
| `DATABASE_URL` | PostgreSQL connection string | Yes |
| `NEXTAUTH_SECRET` | Session encryption key | Yes |

## External Dependencies

{Services this system talks to. For each: what, why, and what happens if it's down.}

| Service | Purpose | Failure Mode |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| PostgreSQL | Primary data store | App down |
| Redis | Session cache, rate limiting | Degraded (falls back to DB) |
| Stripe | Payment processing | Payments fail, app continues |

## Development Workflow

{How to go from a fresh clone to a running system. Be specific — exact commands.}

```bash
# Setup
{exact commands}

# Run
{exact commands}

# Test
{exact commands}
```
```

## Step 3: Validate

After writing, verify:

1. **Every file path mentioned exists** — grep for each path you cited
2. **Every module/class/function mentioned exists** — they may have been renamed
3. **Mermaid diagrams render** — check syntax is valid
4. **Tech stack matches reality** — versions match package.json/pyproject.toml
5. **Entry points are complete** — no missing route files, CLI commands, or workers
6. **Env vars match** — cross-check with .env.example or actual config loading code

## Step 4: Commit (if in Dirigent pipeline)

If running as part of the Dirigent pipeline:
```bash
git add ARCHITECTURE.md && git commit -m "docs: generate ARCHITECTURE.md"
```

If running standalone (user invoked directly), do NOT commit — let the user review first.

## Update Mode (--update)

When `--update` is passed:

1. Read the existing ARCHITECTURE.md
2. For each section, verify claims against current code
3. Update sections that are stale (wrong file paths, missing modules, changed patterns)
4. Preserve sections wrapped in `<!-- manual -->...<!-- /manual -->` — these were hand-written
5. Add new sections for things that didn't exist when the doc was first written
6. Remove sections for things that no longer exist
7. Commit: `git add ARCHITECTURE.md && git commit -m "docs: update ARCHITECTURE.md"`

## Rules

<rules>
<rule>Every claim must be verified against the actual code — never guess or assume</rule>
<rule>File paths must be checked with ls or glob before writing them into the doc</rule>
<rule>Prefer showing 5 important things over listing 50 things exhaustively</rule>
<rule>Mermaid diagrams must use valid syntax — test by reading them back</rule>
<rule>The doc must be useful on day 1 to an agent with zero context — no jargon without explanation</rule>
<rule>If running as part of Dirigent init, always commit. If standalone, never commit.</rule>
<rule>Do not include implementation details that change frequently — focus on structure and patterns</rule>
<rule>For monorepos, show the package/app structure and how packages depend on each other</rule>
</rules>

<constraints>
<constraint>Output is ARCHITECTURE.md at the repo root — one file only</constraint>
<constraint>Maximum 15 minutes — prioritize structure and patterns over exhaustive listing</constraint>
<constraint>Do not read every file — sample strategically (entry points, config, key modules)</constraint>
</constraints>
